title: Association of mortality and combined oxidative capacity of ozone and nitrogen dioxide
authors: Micheline de Sousa Zanotti Stagliorio Coelho
Susana das Neves Pereira da Silva
data_sources: MCC Multi-Country Multi-City Collaborative Research Network
national air-quality monitoring networks (O3 and NO2)

data_scope.exposure_variables: daily O3 and NO2 concentrations (ppb) → Oxwt index
data_scope.outcome_variables: daily total, cardiovascular, and respiratory mortality counts
data_scope.data_access: restricted (MCC data-sharing agreement); code open via Zenodo doi:10.5281/zenodo.20019197

core_conclusion: The combined oxidative capacity of O3 and NO2 (Oxwt) is more strongly associated with daily mortality than either pollutant alone. Across 380 cities in 23 countries (1985–2020), a 10-ppb increase in Oxwt over a 2-day lag corresponds to a 0.82% increase in total mortality, 1.09% in cardiovascular mortality, and 0.88% in respiratory mortality. Attributable deaths under Oxwt exceed those from individual pollutants but are fewer than their sum, confirming non-additive synergy. Oxwt is proposed as a practical combined indicator for public health surveillance.
why_it_matters: First global multi-city quantification of a joint oxidative pollutant indicator; proposes a simple, governance-ready composite metric from routine monitoring data; documents geographic and temporal heterogeneity in the oxidant–mortality relationship; demonstrates that single-pollutant regulatory frameworks systematically undercount the combined mortality burden of O3 and NO2.
why_nature: Sustainability-scale planetary scope linking urban air chemistry to population mortality; novel composite indicator with direct regulatory uptake potential; 35-year global dataset from the MCC Collaborative constitutes landmark evidence synthesis; directly challenges existing WHO and national air-quality standards for individual pollutants.
added_value_to_my_research: City-level Oxwt effect estimates → spatially varying outcome for MGWR to map intra-urban environmental health risk
SHAP-explained gradient boosting on 380 city-level effect estimates → interpretable ranking of structural drivers of effect heterogeneity
Oxwt burden × 2SFCA healthcare-access surfaces → joint access-exposure inequity analysis for environmental justice
Spatiotemporal KDE + satellite-derived NO2/O3 → annual Oxwt exposure surfaces for long-term cohort linkage
EV-adoption and urban-greening causal chains → mortality dividend quantification through Oxwt reduction pathways

related.title: Mortality from short-term exposure to particulate matter, nitrogen dioxide and ozone across Europe
related.relation: directly complementary multi-city European study of the same gaseous pollutants and mortality endpoints

related.title: Reversal of trends in global fine particulate matter air pollution
related.relation: global trend context for ambient air pollutants; benchmark for PM2.5 vs. oxidant regulatory approaches

related.title: Racial/ethnic disparities in PM2.5-attributable cardiovascular mortality burden in the United States
related.relation: methodological template for attributable mortality fraction by social stratum; equity extension of pollutant burden analysis

related.title: Air pollution control strategies directly limiting national health damages in the US
related.relation: causal policy-impact framework for translating pollutant reductions to avoided mortality — directly applicable to Oxwt

related.title: Consumption in the G20 nations causes particulate air pollution resulting in two million premature deaths annually
related.relation: upstream consumption-attribution approach applicable to NO2/O3 sources and Oxwt burden allocation
